narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer despite the challenges many face in this field. Your application included your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 34, rankings in the 2nd decile for both your first-year and third-year school grades, an upper-middle family income background, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 99.2% chance that you would fail it—far higher than what it sees in most applicants. This extremely high predicted risk of bar failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed on the exam right away. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school grade decile of 2, well below the typical 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model weaker performance compared to peers. Your first-year decile of 2 had the next strongest pull in the same direction, again lower than average and reinforcing concerns about consistent academic strength. Your family income in the upper-middle bracket also contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction, as it didn't stand out as a protective factor against risks in this dataset. Even your LSAT score of 34 nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, since it's under the dataset average of around 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 added a small similar influence relative to the typical 3.21. Your choice of full-time study had a minor role, aligning with the vast majority but not enough to offset the others. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having lower-than-average grades and test scores across the board, which outweighed your background strengths in the model's eyes. While this decision is tough, especially knowing the extra hurdles Black women often navigate in legal education, it's not the end of your path. I encourage you to retake the LSAT or bolster your grades through additional coursework, then reapply to programs with strong bar prep support tailored to diverse students like you—many have turned similar predictions around with targeted effort. You've got resilience on your side; keep pushing forward.
